summary_text:
S:
The patient reports constant pain in his left shoulder and left elbow for approximately five months, stemming from a fall five months ago. He describes the pain as constant and quite bad at times. He has been trying to maintain his two-day-a-week gym routine, but the pain makes it difficult to lift more than 15-20 pounds. He has stopped going to the gym for the past one to two weeks due to the pain, which is frustrating. He continues to walk approximately 30 minutes daily with his wife since retiring last year. He denies any numbness or tingling. He has not tried any pain medications or ice, but has iced the area somewhat.

O:
Left shoulder and elbow are tender to palpation, with no warmth, erythema, or deformity. Hawkins-Kennedy and Neer's tests are positive. Proximal and distal upper extremity strength is normal. Median, radial, and ulnar sensation are intact. Abduction is 90 degrees. Emptying the trapezius is normal. No signs of swelling.

A:
The patient is presenting with impingement syndrome of the left shoulder. The fall likely contributed to the current symptoms. There is no evidence of acute wrist injury, but the shoulder pain is impacting the wrist.

P:
I am requesting a referral to Dr. Martha Rivera for physical therapy, starting with two sessions per week. We will monitor his response to physical therapy and consider a cortisone injection if needed. Please continue icing the shoulder and rest as much as possible. I will provide a referral for physical therapy and instruct the patient to call or email if symptoms worsen or do not improve.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Left shoulder and elbow pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Jose James is a 64-year-old male who presents for left shoulder and elbow pain.

The patient reports falling on his hand while going up stairs 5 months ago and has been experiencing constant pain since. He admits he was continuing to complete strength training exercises at least 2 times per week, however, he was unable to lift more than 15-20 pounds due to the pain.

During the past 1-2 weeks, Mr. James has discontinued strength training activities. Additionally, he has iced the inflicted areas, denies taking any pain medications, and reports his pain has slightly improved but it is still rather constant. He denies tingling and numbness.

The patient walks 30-minutes most days of the week.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Retired last year, was an office worker.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Musculoskeletal
Left shoulder and elbow, tender subacromial space. No warmth, erythema, or deformity. Positive Hawkins-Kennedy and Neer’s test. Normal proximal and distal upper extremity strength. Intact median, radial, ulnar sensation. Abduction to 90 degrees. Normal empty can test.

ASSESSMENT

• Impingement syndrome of left shoulder

Jose is a 64-year-old male who was seen today for impingement syndrome of his left shoulder, likely related to a fall he incurred 5 months ago.

PLAN

Today I discussed conservative options for left shoulder impingement with the patient, who opted to try physical therapy 2 session per week. Cortisone injection was discussed as an option if the physical therapy does not successfully reduce his pain. Additionally, I recommended continued ice and rest.

INSTRUCTIONS

email, or call if symptoms worsen or do not resolve.

section_miscategorizations.reason: The content is appropriate for the Plan, but the first-person phrasing ('I am requesting') is more typical of an Assessment/plan narrative than a discrete plan order; however, this does not substantially distort meaning.
